headline: Interfaith Dialogue Commission Proposed 4 Times, Rejected 4 Times
context: Proposals for a National Interfaith Dialogue Commission were tabled in 2001, 2005, 2013, and 2023, each time withdrawn after opposition from religious conservatives.

cards.big: Malaysia has tried to create an interfaith commission 4 times in 22 years.
cards.sub: Each attempt was abandoned. The country has no formal mechanism for inter-religious dispute resolution.

cards.big: Article 11(3)(a) allows each religion to manage its own affairs.
cards.sub: No provision addresses disputes between religions — custody, conversion, burial — leaving them in legal limbo.

cards.big: 74% of Malaysians support a formal interfaith dialogue mechanism.
cards.sub: A 2024 survey found majority support across all ethnic groups, including 62% of Malay respondents.

cards.big: Indonesia's Forum Kerukunan Umat Beragama has operated since 2006.
cards.sub: Malaysia remains the only Muslim-majority ASEAN nation without a formal interfaith institution.

cards.big: 4 proposals in 22 years, all withdrawn. The political system cannot deliver what 74% of the public supports.
Interfaith disputes do not disappear because we lack a commission. They go to courts ill-equipped to resolve them, or remain unresolved. The cost of inaction is borne by families.
